preamble.summary: A thanksgiving psalm celebrating rescue from the brink of death. The superscription associates it with the dedication of the house (the temple or David's palace). David praises the LORD for pulling him up from Sheol, recounts his complacency before the crisis ('I said in my prosperity, I shall never be moved'), confesses that God hid his face and he was dismayed, and then declares that God has turned his mourning into dancing and clothed him with joy.
preamble.remarkable: The psalm's emotional honesty is arresting. David admits that prosperity made him complacent — 'In my ease I said: I will never be shaken' (v. 7). The Hebrew word shalvi ('my ease, my security, my prosperity') describes the dangerous comfort that comes from extended blessing. When everything goes well for long enough, the human heart starts to believe it deserves stability. Then God hid his face (histarta fanekha, v. 8), and the foundation collapsed. The psalm teaches that the prosperity itself was God's gift, and its removal was God's pedagogy. The most quoted line — 'Weeping may lodge for the night, but joy comes in the morning' (v. 6) — compresses an entire theology of suffering into a single sentence: sorrow is temporary and nocturnal; joy is permanent and diurnal. Darkness is the visitor; light is the resident.
preamble.friction: The superscription — mizmor shir chanukat ha-bayit le-David ('a psalm, a song for the dedication of the house, of David') — creates historical puzzlement. David did not build or dedicate the temple; Solomon did (1 Kings 8). The 'house' (bayit) may refer to David's palace (2 Samuel 5:11), or the superscription may indicate liturgical use at the later temple dedication, or at the Feast of Hanukkah (which takes its name from chanukah, 'dedication'). The psalm is indeed read during Hanukkah in Jewish tradition. The phrase 'you brought my soul up from Sheol' (v. 4) could be literal (near-death illness or battle wound) or figurative (a crisis so severe it felt like death). The psalm does not specify the nature of the crisis, which makes it universally applicable.
preamble.connections: The descent-to-Sheol and rescue pattern connects to Jonah 2 (prayer from the belly of the fish), Psalm 18:4-6, and Psalm 116:3-8. The 'mourning into dancing' transformation (v. 12) echoes Jeremiah 31:13 ('I will turn their mourning into joy'). The 'morning joy' of verse 6 anticipates Lamentations 3:22-23 ('his mercies are new every morning'). The theological pattern — prosperity leading to complacency, crisis restoring dependence — runs through Deuteronomy 8:11-18 and Hosea 13:6.

verses.text_kjv: A Psalm and Song at the dedication of the house of David.
verses.rendering: A psalm — a song for the dedication of the house. Of David.
translator_notes: The superscription combines mizmor ('psalm,' a musical composition) with shir ('song,' a vocal piece) — this is both instrumental and vocal. Chanukat ha-bayit ('the dedication of the house') uses the word chanukah, from which the Jewish festival takes its name. The 'house' is ambiguous — David's palace, the temple site, or a future liturgical context. In WLC versification, this superscription is verse 1.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲרוֹמִמְךָ֣ יְ֭הוָה כִּ֣י דִלִּיתָ֑נִי וְלֹא־שִׂמַּ֖חְתָּ אֹיְבַ֣י לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I will extol thee, O LORD; for thou hast lifted me up, and hast not made my foes to rejoice over me.
verses.rendering:
I will exalt you, O LORD, for you have drawn me up
and have not let my enemies gloat over me.
verses.expanded_rendering: Aromimkha ('I will lift you up, exalt you') responds to dillitani ('you drew me up, you lifted me'). The verbal echo is deliberate: God lifted the speaker, so the speaker lifts God. The verb dalah ('to draw up') is the verb for drawing water from a well — the speaker was at the bottom of a deep shaft, and God lowered the bucket and pulled him out. The second blessing: velo simmachta oyvai li ('you did not make my enemies rejoice over me'). Rescue from death included rescue from humiliation. The enemies who expected to celebrate the speaker's destruction were denied their triumph.
translator_notes: Dalah ('to draw up') from a cistern or well creates a vivid image of depth and rescue. The bor ('pit') of Psalm 28:1 and the Sheol of verse 4 are the deep places from which God draws the speaker up. The well metaphor implies darkness, confinement, and helplessness at the bottom.

verses.text_kjv: O LORD my God, I cried unto thee, and thou hast healed me.
verses.rendering:
O LORD my God, I cried to you for help,
and you healed me.
translator_notes: Vattirpaeni ('and you healed me') uses the verb rapa ('to heal, cure, restore'). This suggests the crisis may have been illness — or the word may be used broadly for any restoration of well-being. Either way, the pattern is simple and complete: I cried (shivvati), you healed (tirpaeni). The brevity mirrors the experience — when God acts, the transformation is swift.

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה הֶ֭עֱלִיתָ מִן־שְׁא֣וֹל נַפְשִׁ֑י חִ֝יִּיתַ֗נִי מִיּ֥וֹרְדִי־בֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD, thou hast brought up my soul from the grave: thou hast kept me alive, that I should not go down to the pit.
verses.rendering:
O LORD, you brought my soul up from Sheol;
you kept me alive from among those going down to the pit.
verses.expanded_rendering: He'elita min-Sheol nafshi ('you brought up my soul from Sheol') — the speaker was so close to death that he describes himself as already there. Sheol is the realm of the dead, the underworld, the place of no return. Yet God brought him up (he'elita, from alah, 'to go up, ascend') — a vertical rescue from the deepest possible location. Chiyyitani miyyordei-vor ('you kept me alive from among those descending to the pit') means God pulled the speaker out of the line of people marching into death. He was in the queue for the grave, and God removed him.
translator_notes: The preposition min ('from') in min-Sheol could mean 'from Sheol' (he was already there) or 'from going to Sheol' (he was headed there). Either way, the proximity to death was extreme. The speaker does not overstate the crisis — he uses the strongest possible language because the crisis was genuinely life-threatening.

verses.text_hebrew: זַמְּר֣וּ לַיהוָ֣ה חֲסִידָ֑יו וְ֝הוֹד֗וּ לְזֵ֣כֶר קׇדְשֽׁוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Sing unto the LORD, O ye saints of his, and give thanks at the remembrance of his holiness.
verses.rendering:
Sing praise to the LORD, you his faithful ones,
and give thanks to his holy name.
translator_notes: Chasidav ('his faithful ones, his devoted ones') — those who are bound to God by chesed, who have received and reciprocated God's covenant love. They are summoned to zammeru ('sing, make music') and hodu ('give thanks, confess, acknowledge'). The phrase zekher qodsho ('the remembrance/memorial of his holiness' or 'his holy name') treats God's holiness as something to be commemorated and declared publicly.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י רֶ֨גַע ׀ בְּאַפּוֹ֮ חַיִּ֢ים בִּרְצ֫וֹנ֥וֹ בָּ֭עֶרֶב יָלִ֣ין בֶּ֑כִי וְ֝לַבֹּ֗קֶר רִנָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: For his anger endureth but a moment; in his favour is life: weeping may endure for a night, but joy cometh in the morning.
verses.rendering:
For his anger lasts only a moment,
but his favor lasts a lifetime.
Weeping may lodge for the night,
but joy comes in the morning.
verses.expanded_rendering: One of the most quoted verses in the Psalter, and deservedly so. The structure is chiastic: anger (rega, 'a moment') contrasts with favor (ratson, 'acceptance, pleasure, favor') which brings chayyim ('life, a lifetime'). Then the time-image: ba-erev yalin bekhi ('in the evening weeping lodges') — weeping is personified as a traveler who checks into the inn at nightfall. It stays the night. But ve-la-boqer rinnah ('and at the morning, a shout of joy') — joy arrives at dawn like the sun. Weeping is the overnight guest; joy is the permanent resident. The theology is profoundly hopeful without being dismissive of suffering: the psalm does not deny that weeping happens; it insists that weeping is temporary. Night always yields to morning. Anger always gives way to favor. The proportions are radically asymmetric: a moment of anger, a lifetime of favor.
translator_notes: Rega ('a moment, an instant') is the smallest unit of time the Hebrew language can express. God's anger occupies a rega; God's favor occupies chayyim ('a whole life'). The disproportion is the point — wrath is a flicker; grace is the main event.
The verb yalin ('to lodge, to spend the night') applied to weeping treats sorrow as a temporary visitor. Rinnah ('a shout of joy, a ringing cry') is not quiet relief but vocal, public jubilation.

verses.text_kjv: And in my prosperity I said, I shall never be moved.
verses.rendering:
As for me, I said in my ease:
"I will never be shaken."
verses.expanded_rendering: The confession. Va-ani amarti be-shalvi ('and I, I said in my ease/prosperity') — shalvah means 'ease, quiet, security, complacency.' It is the condition of someone who has been blessed long enough to forget the source of the blessing. The declaration bal-emmot le-olam ('I will not be shaken forever') is the language of false permanence — the speaker mistook God's current favor for his own inherent stability. This is the theological error the psalm diagnoses: the belief that because things are good now, they will always be good, as though goodness were a personal possession rather than a divine gift. The admission is startlingly honest: the speaker confesses his own spiritual complacency as a contributing factor to the crisis that followed.
translator_notes: The phrase bal-emmot ('I will not be shaken') echoes Psalm 21:8 and Psalm 16:8 — but there the stability comes from trusting in God, while here it comes from self-confidence born of prosperity. The same words carry opposite theological weight depending on their source.

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה בִּ֭רְצוֹנְךָ הֶעֱמַ֣דְתָּ לְהַרְרִ֣י עֹ֑ז הִסְתַּ֥רְתָּ פָ֝נֶ֗יךָ הָיִ֥יתִי נִבְהָֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: LORD, by thy favour thou hast made my mountain to stand strong: thou didst hide thy face, and I was troubled.
verses.rendering:
O LORD, by your favor you made my mountain stand strong;
you hid your face — I was dismayed.
verses.expanded_rendering: Two phases compressed into one verse. Phase one: bi-retsonkha heemadta le-harri oz ('by your favor you established for my mountain strength') — God's favor (ratson) was the hidden cause of the speaker's stability. The 'mountain' (har) is the speaker's life, position, or kingdom, made strong by God's will. Phase two: histarta fanekha ('you hid your face') — and immediately, hayiti nivhal ('I was terrified, dismayed, shattered'). The verb bahal means 'to be disturbed, alarmed, terrified, shattered.' The transition from security to terror required only one action: God hiding his face. Everything the speaker thought was his own — his mountain, his strength, his permanence — was actually God's face turned toward him. When the face turned away, everything collapsed. The lesson: what felt like personal strength was always reflected divine favor.
translator_notes: Histarta fanekha ('you hid your face') is one of the most frightening phrases in the Psalter. The hidden face of God means the withdrawal of attention, favor, protection, and presence. It is the experience behind Psalm 22:1 ('why have you forsaken me?') and behind every dark night of the soul. Here, it serves a pedagogical purpose: God hid his face to show the speaker what his life was like without it.

verses.text_kjv: I cried to thee, O LORD; and unto the LORD I made supplication.
verses.rendering:
To you, O LORD, I called,
and to the Lord I pleaded for mercy.
translator_notes: Two names — YHWH and Adonai ('Lord, Master') — and two verbs — eqra ('I called') and etchannan ('I pleaded for grace/mercy'). The shift from complacent self-confidence (v. 7) to desperate prayer (v. 9) is the crisis itself: the speaker who said 'I will never be shaken' is now begging for mercy. The complacency had to break before the prayer could begin.

verses.text_hebrew: מַה־בֶּ֥צַע בְּדָמִ֗י בְּ֭רִדְתִּי אֶל־שָׁ֑חַת הֲיוֹדְךָ֥ עָ֝פָ֗ר הֲיַגִּ֥יד אֲמִתֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: What profit is there in my blood, when I go down to the pit? Shall the dust praise thee? shall it declare thy truth?
verses.rendering:
What profit is there in my death,
in my going down to the pit?
Can the dust praise you?
Can it declare your faithfulness?
verses.expanded_rendering: The speaker's argument to God is pragmatic and almost audacious: mah betsa be-dami ('what profit is there in my blood?'). If I die, what do you gain? The dead cannot praise God — ha-yodekha afar ('can the dust thank you?') — and the dust cannot declare God's faithfulness — ha-yaggid amittekha ('can it tell your truth?'). The argument assumes that God wants to be praised and that living worshippers serve God's purposes better than dead ones. This is not selfish manipulation but honest theological reasoning: a God who delights in praise has a vested interest in keeping his worshippers alive. The shachat ('pit, corruption, grave') is the place of silence where no song reaches God's ears.
translator_notes: This argument — the dead cannot praise God — appears repeatedly in the Psalter (Psalm 6:5, 88:10-12, 115:17). It reflects a pre-resurrection understanding where Sheol is a place of silence and diminished existence. The speaker leverages this theology: keep me alive so I can keep worshipping.

verses.text_kjv: Hear, O LORD, and have mercy upon me: LORD, be thou my helper.
verses.rendering:
Hear, O LORD, and be gracious to me!
O LORD, be my helper!
translator_notes: Three imperatives in rapid succession: shema ('hear'), chonneni ('be gracious to me'), heyeh ozer li ('be a helper to me'). The brevity reflects urgency — this is not elaborate liturgical prayer but compressed crisis communication. Every word is essential.

verses.text_hebrew: הָפַ֣כְתָּ מִסְפְּדִי֮ לְמָח֢וֹל לִ֥י פִּתַּ֥חְתָּ שַׂקִּ֑י וַֽתְּאַזְּרֵ֥נִי שִׂמְחָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast turned for me my mourning into dancing: thou hast put off my sackcloth, and girded me with gladness;
verses.expanded_rendering: The transformation is total and tactile. Hafakhta ('you turned, you reversed, you overturned') — the verb of radical inversion. God did not merely improve the speaker's situation; he flipped it entirely. Mispedi ('my mourning, my wailing') becomes machol ('dancing, a round dance'). Sackcloth (saq — the coarse garment of grief, worn against the skin as a physical expression of inner anguish) is stripped off (pittachta, 'you opened, you loosened') and replaced with simchah ('joy, gladness') as a garment tied around the speaker (vatte'azzreni, 'you girded me'). Joy is not merely an emotion; it is something God wraps around the speaker like a robe. The clothing imagery makes the transformation visible — everyone can see the change. Where sackcloth announced grief to the world, the garment of joy announces God's deliverance.
translator_notes: The reversal pairs are precise: mourning/dancing, sackcloth/joy, removal/clothing. Each pair represents a complete inversion — not gradual improvement but sudden, dramatic transformation. This is the 'morning joy' of verse 6 made concrete in the speaker's wardrobe.

verses.text_hebrew: לְמַ֤עַן ׀ יְזַמֶּרְךָ֣ כָ֭בוֹד וְלֹ֣א יִדֹּ֑ם יְהוָ֥ה אֱ֝לֹהַ֗י לְעוֹלָ֥ם אוֹדֶֽךָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: To the end that my glory may sing praise to thee, and not be silent. O LORD my God, I will give thanks unto thee for ever.
verses.rendering:
so that my glory may sing your praise and not be silent.
O LORD my God, I will give thanks to you forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse answers the question of verse 10: can the dust praise you? No — but the living speaker can, and will. Leman yezamerkha khavod ('so that glory may sing to you') — the word kavod here likely refers to the speaker's own glory, his soul, his honor, the highest part of his being. God rescued him so that his kavod could sing rather than be silenced by death. Velo yiddom ('and not be silent') — the silence of the grave (v. 10) is averted; the speaker will never stop praising. YHWH Elohai le-olam odekka ('LORD my God, forever I will thank you') — the final word is commitment to permanent gratitude. The psalm began with near-death and ends with le-olam ('forever') — the speaker's thanksgiving will outlast the crisis that prompted it.
translator_notes: Kavod ('glory') as a reference to the speaker's soul or inner being appears also in Psalm 16:9 ('my glory rejoices') and Psalm 57:8 ('awake, my glory'). The human kavod is the reflection of divine kavod — the part of a person capable of worship. To silence it through death would be to extinguish a flame that was meant to burn in praise.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, honor, weight, heaviness, soul, inner being, reputation
key_terms.note: Here kavod refers to the speaker's own glory — the dignity, honor, and worshipping capacity that God created in him. The purpose of rescue from death is not merely survival but restored worship. God saves kavod so that kavod can sing.
